source.title: Old Brains Come Uncoupled in Sleep: Slow Wave-Spindle Synchrony, Brain Atrophy, and Forgetting

confidence_rationale: Healthy adult human cohort with structural MRI and high-density EEG during sleep. Selective atrophy within *medial frontal cortex* specifically -- not a generic frontal atrophy and not other cortical regions -- predicted temporal dispersion of slow wave-spindle coupling and overnight memory forgetting. This is the strongest available human evidence that the MECH-261 write gate has a specific cortical locus-of-selection, not a generic cortical substrate. Correlational rather than causal, hence the moderate confidence, but the anatomical specificity of the atrophy-to-dispersion link makes the mapping to SD-033a concrete.
failure_signatures: Atrophy in non-medial-frontal regions (occipital, parietal, temporal controls) did not predict SO-spindle decoupling. The gate-selection property is spatially localised to MFC; a MECH-261 implementation that treats the gate as spatially diffuse contradicts this.

The direction of the effect was slow-oscillation-governs-spindle (not the reverse). A REE implementation that schedules spindle-events before respecting a slow-oscillation carrier phase will produce the same temporal dispersion pattern seen in older adults and fail to consolidate.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The slow oscillation causally governs the precise temporal coordination of sleep spindles; this coupling quality predicts overnight memory retention; selective atrophy within medial frontal cortex in older adults disperses this coupling and impairs memory consolidation.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-261's per-mode write gate has a specific biological locus-of-selection, not a distributed one. In SWS the locus is medial frontal cortex, which is the rodent-primate-common homologue for REE's SD-033a lateral-PFC-analog write target. Atrophy of the locus, not of the cortical substrate generally, breaks the gate: the mode signal is no longer routed with precision onto the write target. In REE terms, this predicts that damaging SD-033a directly should dissolve the mode-selectivity of writes driven by SalienceCoordinator; ablating other SD-033 subdivisions should not.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Correlational human aging study, not a causal manipulation. The atrophy-dispersion link is tight and the regional specificity is strong, but the claim 'MFC *is* the locus-of-selection' is a strong interpretation of a correlational finding. Helfrich are careful to say MFC atrophy predicts dispersion; they do not claim MFC is the sole substrate for the coupling. Treat this as supporting anatomical-specificity evidence layered onto the Maingret causal mPFC result.
mapping.source_context: Healthy older and younger human adults; structural MRI for regional atrophy; high-density scalp EEG during overnight polysomnography; word-pair memory task with pre-sleep learning and next-morning recall.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (Neuron, careful regional controls, large-ish cohort, cross-modal design). Mapping fidelity good on the anatomical-specificity axis. Transfer risk 0.30 because scalp-EEG spindle-SO coupling is a reduced measurement of the underlying thalamocortical circuit, and because the extrapolation from human MFC to SD-033a lateral-PFC-analog involves the same rodent-primate homology issue flagged in SD-033's design doc.
